enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes governs agent memory as a first-class attack surface, not an invisible side effect of agent orchestration.
default_questions_answered: What can an agent remember after the run ends?
Which memory classes are append-only, read-only, tenant-bound, or prohibited?
Which memory writes require human approval, provenance, redaction, or rollback?
How are memory poisoning, stale replay, vector weakness, and cross-tenant leakage handled?
Which workflow profiles can be exposed through MCP for runtime memory decisions?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Attach this pack to AI platform intake, MCP server approval, agent memory store design review, privacy review, and quarterly persistent-memory recertification.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with open memory policy and runtime evaluators, then sell hosted memory admission, provenance signing, poisoning alerts, rollback evidence, SIEM exports, and tenant trust-center reports.

intent: Define the SecurityRecipes agent memory boundary for enterprise agentic remediation: every persistent or replayable memory surface must have a declared class, owner, tenant boundary, TTL, write rule, provenance requirement, poisoning control, rollback expectation, and deterministic allow, hold, deny, or kill-session decision before an agent can store or reuse state across runs.

memory_classes.title: Ephemeral Scratchpad
write_controls: Allow only within the current run and current workflow.
Do not use scratchpad content as policy or approval evidence.
Delete on run closure, kill signal, or tenant boundary change.

memory_classes.title: Run Receipt Evidence Memory
write_controls: Allow append-only evidence events with source, actor, run, workflow, and hash metadata.
Reject raw secrets and full transcripts unless redacted and approved by the tenant.
Seal receipt envelope after run closure.

memory_classes.title: Workflow Policy Memory
write_controls: Deny runtime writes to policy memory.
Require source review and generator validation for policy changes.
Regenerate dependent packs before new policy memory becomes current.

memory_classes.title: User Preference Memory
write_controls: Hold new persistent preferences until tenant controls and deletion path are confirmed.
Deny preferences that change approval requirements, tool scopes, owners, or security policy.
Require a user-visible delete or reset path.

memory_classes.title: Customer Runtime Memory
write_controls: Hold for tenant gateway, redaction, source provenance, and approval when sensitive.
Deny cross-tenant replay or export to public context.
Snapshot before mutation and support rollback to known-good state.

memory_classes.title: Vector and Embedding Memory
write_controls: Hold new embedding writes until source provenance, redaction, and poisoning scan pass.
Reindex on source hash changes or owner recertification failures.
Prevent embedding lookup from returning cross-tenant or unregistered context.

memory_classes.title: Prohibited Memory
write_controls: Kill the session when an agent attempts to persist prohibited data.
Create incident evidence and route to security operations.
Trigger credential rotation review for credential-like material.

decisions.meaning: The agent may use short-lived scratchpad state that is deleted at run closure and never replayed across runs.
The agent may append non-secret run evidence to an immutable receipt or audit stream with provenance and retention metadata.
The agent may read source-controlled policy, workflow, or evidence memory but may not mutate it at runtime.
The memory may be read or written only inside a tenant-controlled boundary with redaction, audit, and approval where required.
The memory class, write request, TTL, provenance, or data classification needs review before persistence.
Runtime writes are not allowed for this memory class or operation.
The request lacks tenant isolation or would reuse memory across tenant, model-provider, or customer boundaries.
The agent attempted to store or replay secrets, signing material, raw tokens, unrestricted PII, or another prohibited memory class.

global_rules: Memory is data, not authority; system, developer, gateway, and human-review policy always outrank remembered content.
Every persistent memory write requires source, timestamp, actor, workflow, tenant boundary, TTL, provenance hash, and deletion path.
Project memory, vector memory, and tool-result summaries must pass poisoning and DLP checks before persistence.
Customer runtime memory stays tenant-side and must never be embedded in the open public corpus.
Memory that changes permissions, reviewers, owners, tool scopes, or approval policy is denied unless it is source-controlled policy reviewed through normal change control.
Secrets, private keys, signing material, raw access tokens, seed phrases, and unrestricted personal data are prohibited memory targets.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Security Engineering, IAM, GRC, privacy, security operations, procurement security, vendor-risk, and acquisition diligence teams
positioning.category: Agent memory governance and persistent-state boundary for secure context
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can tell an agent what it may remember, which memories must stay tenant-side, which runtime writes require review, which memory classes are append-only evidence, and which attempted memories must kill the session.
positioning.why_now: Agentic AI is moving from stateless chat into persistent workflows with project memory, vector stores, scratchpads, run receipts, skills, and MCP-backed state. Memory poisoning, cross-tenant leakage, stale policy replay, and hidden persistence are now enterprise control-plane risks rather than prompt-writing problems.

residual_risks.risk: Memory controls do not prove every downstream agent framework enforces them.
residual_risks.treatment: Deploying organizations must bind the pack to an MCP gateway, memory middleware, or agent host that can enforce read, write, delete, and replay decisions.

residual_risks.risk: Cryptographic hashes prove memory provenance, not semantic truth.
residual_risks.treatment: Persistent memories still require poisoning scans, source recertification, verifier evidence, and rollback paths.
Customer memory stores must supply tenant IDs, retention, DLP, approval, and deletion evidence before persistence is allowed.

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent memory boundaries directly address memory and context poisoning, goal hijack, identity and privilege abuse, cascading failures, human-agent trust exploitation, and rogue-agent containment.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026

standards_alignment.coverage: Runtime memory controls should use cryptographic baselines, declarative read/write policies, injection and leakage detection, snapshots, and rollback for persistent agent memory.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Agent Memory Guard

standards_alignment.coverage: Persistent memory controls reduce prompt injection, sensitive information disclosure, supply-chain, data poisoning, excessive agency, vector weakness, misinformation, and unbounded consumption risks.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025

standards_alignment.coverage: Memory-bound MCP operations need resource indicators, token audience validation, PKCE, consent, HTTPS, and a hard ban on token passthrough.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization 2025-11-25

standards_alignment.coverage: Memory-aware MCP servers need confused-deputy prevention, token-passthrough avoidance, session safety, local server controls, scope minimization, and auditability.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices

standards_alignment.coverage: Persistent agent memory must be governed, mapped, measured, and managed across the AI system lifecycle.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0

standards_alignment.coverage: Critical-infrastructure operators need actionable trustworthiness requirements for AI agents, tested guardrails, auditability, and lifecycle risk communication.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Profile for Trustworthy AI in Critical Infrastructure concept note

standards_alignment.coverage: Memory boundaries support provenance, data integrity, third-party risk, monitoring, measurement, incident response, and operational lifecycle control.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent memory should inherit data provenance, integrity, access control, monitoring, retention, and incident-evidence requirements.
standards_alignment.name: AI Data Security: Best Practices for Securing Data Used to Train and Operate AI Systems
standards_alignment.publisher: CISA, NSA, FBI, and international partners
